result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only shows up after something ends.

Not the immediate silence—no, that one is loud, full of ringing and debris. I mean the quiet that settles days later, when the world has already decided to keep going without asking your permission. The kind that lingers in small places: the extra mug you don’t need anymore, the chair that stays tucked in, the message you almost send before remembering there’s nowhere for it to land.

In that quiet, time behaves strangely. Minutes stretch like soft taffy, but whole weeks vanish without leaving footprints. You start noticing things you never did before: the exact pitch of your refrigerator hum, the way sunlight pauses on the same corner of the floor every afternoon, how your own footsteps sound when they don’t have anyone else to harmonize with.

It’s not all heavy, though. That’s the trick of it.

Because somewhere inside that quiet, there’s also space. Unclaimed, a little awkward, but real. Space where your thoughts don’t have to negotiate. Space where your habits can loosen their collars. Space where you can try on a slightly different version of yourself without anyone asking, “since when?”

At first, that space feels like standing on an empty stage after the audience has gone home. Too big. Too exposed. But if you stay there long enough, your eyes adjust. You start to see the edges, the possibilities. You move a little. Then more. You realize the stage isn’t just where something ended—it’s also where something else might begin, even if you don’t yet know the script.

And that’s the quiet nobody warns you about.

Not the absence.

The opening.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
